speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 1, 1996. Survivor's name is William Cohn. Last name at birth is Cohn. Interviewer, Catherine Dickson. City and state, Dallas, Texas. Country, United States of America. Language, English. Today the date is December 1, 1996. I'm interviewing Mr. William Cohn. We are interviewing in Dallas, Texas, United States of America. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you repeat your name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Catherine Dickson.

speaker: interviewer
text: There we go. OK. Let's go. Come on back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please state your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is William Cohn. Generally known as Willi Cohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your last name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-O-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same. Oh, the first name? The-- yeah. William. Wilhelm in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that spelled differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was spelled W-I-L-H-E-L-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the way you're currently spelling your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The way you currently spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-L-L-I-A-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names that you went by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I was at a training farm of the Zionist movement to be prepared for immigration to, at the time, Palestine, I was given a Hebrew name which was Seew and spelled S-E-E-W. And it means, wolf. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And before that, I had to change my family name because [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] before I went to-- before I was sent to the training farm in Italy, I worked for a forester, worked far from our home town, to be trained as a-- to work as a forestry apprentice. But he could not take me in as a Jew, so I had to change my name. And I changed it to Conrad, C-O-N-R-A-D. That was a cover name, so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 4, 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city is Stendal. That's S-T-E-N-D-A-L. The same as the French author, but he spelt his name-- he spelled his name with an 'h,' which means the 'd' and the 'a' he put an 'h.' And that wasn't his real name. He took it because he admired a classical research-- researcher that really started to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] probably say this again, the classics. Especially the Greek classics, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Has the name of Winckelmann, and he was born in my town. And the country is Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born the first year of the First World War. And what I remember of it is actually after, at the end of the First World War, there-- the disillusioned soldiers, especially the sailors of the [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] war ships mutinied and it developed into revolution to overthrow the monarchy. And in our town, I remember, that they burned down the post office, which we could see from our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when they burned down the post office?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four-- three. Three years old, yeah. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was a young age. I don't really remember that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember the death of my grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was put up in our best room. And I remember all the flowers and the greens, you know? And I still remember the smell of it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What else would you like to know? What was your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresa. Theresa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cohn. Married Cohn. Her maiden name was Figenburg. No, sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home life was very close family. But a lot of love, for my mother especially. And I became very close to my mother. And I loved her very much. And my father was kind of critical. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he was a typical-- although he was Jewish, of course-- but he was very much in the Prussian, the Prussian German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember my mother used to tell us when she traveled to Berlin where my father worked as a woodsmaker. And she was-- he met a traveling salesman in Berlin, also a Jewish salesman. And he showed him a picture of my mother. And my father fell in love with the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother had to come to Berlin to meet this man. And when she left her home toward the south of Germany, her family said to her, don't-- just don't bring out a Prussian. And that's exactly what she brought. [LAUGHS] How did the traveling salesman get your mother's picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the salesmen get your mother's picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was her brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother was a half-orphan. Her father had died years before. I don't know when. And so they went looking for a husband, probably for her, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes. And otherwise our family life-- my parents had a store [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] which my father took over from my grandfather, who had started working in a very small, little store in a one-story building with a tipped roof. And they lived in the back of the store. And it was a very small store. And when my grandfather died, my father came back from Berlin and to take over the store. And the landlord of that house made an agreement with my father that he would put up a new three-story, modern building on the condition that my father would enlarge the store. And that's what he did. And the store became quite a sizable store, relatively, to the small town. A very well-known store. And my father and my mother worked together in the store. And we had a maid that cooked and cleaned and lived in the same house, upstairs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] then I remember being sent to kindergarten. My mother had to push me. And I cried first time, first day when she left me alone there. [LAUGHS] And then I went to public school. And in Germany, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] all kids go first public school and until the age of 10. And then the people, the children of families who could afford the tuition for the high school, which was oriented towards university studies. And it was part of the educational-- of the national educational [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] system. But they charged tuition. In Germany, people-- especially the working class-- they couldn't afford it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at 10, I started what was called the Gymnasium. Gymnasium. And it was the name of that-- of those high schools. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there I went through the age of 18. That was the highest grade. The grades were called Latin names, you know, because [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] of the school and the atmosphere of this [INAUDIBLE]. So the highest-- the lowest grade was Sexta. This means six. And the highest grade was the Prima, Uberprima, Upper Prima. That was the highest grade. And then we had to pass [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] examination, which would give us what we needed to enter university.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just four weeks before the examination, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was forced to leave the school, so I couldn't take the examination.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you forced to leave the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a long story. Can I drink? Yeah? [DRINKS] I was very good in art, especially in drawing. And I made-- at that time the political situation was very [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] hectic and violent. And traditionally, the Jews were-- either left-wing-- left-wing

speaker: interviewee
text: communists, socialists, social democrats, democrats. My father was a social democrat, card-carrying social democrat. And so I was even further to the left at that time. I was already 18-- 17, 18 years old. And I made cartoons, political cartoons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I had-- I hadn't have them published. I just made them for my own pleasure, you know, because I liked to draw. And I liked to draw-- to do cartoons. But you know, like the style which you see in or in the daily news you-- every-- on these op-ed papers, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had two sketch books, the same often. And I carried them always in my satchel. And one day, we were-- they disappeared out of my satchel. Because everybody knew these cartoons. And it was before Hitler took power. So nominally, Germany was still a democratic country, yes, so I wasn't to avoid that everybody knew them. And so when they were stolen, I knew who had stolen them. It was the top Nazi in our class. Many of the students were Nazis because they came from the middle classes. And many of the middle classes supported the Nazis. And I knew that he had taken them. And I was told that he had [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] given them to the Nazi headquarter of our town. And that was in fall of 1932. And I wanted to send those sketches to Munich to a friend who knew the most famous cartoonist in Germany at the time. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. And I wanted to know that are these drawings were good enough for me that I could go into this profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were stolen just a few days before I was ready to mail them. So I sat down and, from memory, made most of them again. And then I sent them away. And then I go to the reply from Mr. [INAUDIBLE] saying, that the drawings were very good. No, the ideas were very good, but the drawings needed more polishing to be published. So that-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I was very happy about that. Because I thought maybe I could make it in that profession doing art all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- in the meantime, that was in '32. In fall of '32. And in '33, on January 30th, 1933, Hitler took power and he started right away to persecute all opposition. And when it came to the examinations, they were to me in April, I think, and in March I was called to the principal's office. And I already knew what it would be about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told that my drawings went up all the way to the Ministry of the Interior of Prussia. And they were with Goring, Hermann Goring, at the time. And he was known to be very jovial, you know. He was a fat guy and he was very jovial. He was known for being very jovial on the outside. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the principal said that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Minister of the Interior, Goring, was very amused by those drawings, but that this could not be tolerated. And so my principal said that he would advise me to leave, to not to take the examination, to withdraw from the examinations. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he indicated that otherwise it would not be good for me. And I knew what that meant. It meant concentration camp. So I left the school just before the examination. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that is-- yeah. That is after the [INAUDIBLE]. I went to the forester to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get back your original pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father burned them when they started to do house searches. They didn't need a warrant. They didn't need a search warrant. They just end at a home and search the home. Not only of Jews, but of all suspected opposition people. And so my father got worried. And one night, he took them down to the basement and burned them in the boiler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the pictures that he burned-- which set of pictures were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second. The second set.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first set of pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never know what about them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that it was a schoolmate that took your pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I find out? I didn't have to. I knew that he was. And he was rabid. He was really a rabid Nazi and anti-Semite. And he was the top guy in our class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of non-Jewish students at your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The vast majority. We were only-- we were about 900 students in that school and only four Jewish boys, four Jewish students. Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism within the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Especially the last years, you know, when the Nazi party really grew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes. I once had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a teacher, a history teacher, who had been a combat officer in the First World War. And he was very-- he was feared, you know. Because he was so threatening violence, you know. And everybody was scared of him, not only the Jewish kids, but the others, too, you know. And he became a Nazi. And one time [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he started to teach the racial theory of Jewish inferiority and other inferiority of other races and the superiority of the Aryan race and other classes. So I stood up and started to argue with him. And he got more and more angry and mad, you know. And he swelled up. [LAUGHS] He swelled up. His whole face became red, you know. And then he ran out at the end of the-- and everybody was sitting with their heads on the desk. And I was scared, too. But I couldn't keep my mouth shut, and so-- And afterwards, he ran out of the class when the class was over. Some screaming, something about Jews or something. And then this principal, afterwards I was called to him. He told me that afterwards he went to my records and he would put a commendatory note in my records.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's always kind of a German character, you know. He was mad. He was-- he disagreed, of course, with me, but somehow he respected the courage that I stood up. That I stood up to him. Did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and there were other incidents with other students. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this teacher ever say anything to you about what was written in your records?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the teacher that you had the argument with, did he ever say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have two minutes left.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop and take a tape break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
